summary:
Daniel Dennett, “Where Am I?”
This article is an extended thought-experiment, meant to help the reader think philosophically about issues involving personal identity and survival. Here’s the basic idea.
Dennett describes an important job for which he was recruited by the Pentagon. They want him to go underground to retrieve a missile that got lodged under Tulsa, Oklahoma, and because the missile is extremely radioactive, they want him to leave his brain behind. (This is a fictional story, by the way.) He agrees and undergoes an operation to have his brain removed, placed in a vat, and connected via radio links to his body. He wakes up, goes to visit his brain floating in the vat, and he asks, “Where am I?”
It’s a tough question, given that his brain is in the vat at which he is now looking and his body is standing outside the vat looking in at his brain. He begins to think about the question philosophically, and gives names to his brain and his body to keep things straight. He names his bodiless brain “Yorick” and his brainless body “Hamlet.” He knows where Yorick is—in the vat. And he knows where Hamlet is—outside the vat. But the question is, Where’s Dennett? He runs through three possibilities: (1) Where Hamlet goes, there goes Dennett; (2) Where Yorick goes, there goes Dennett; and (3) Dennett is wherever he thinks he is. The problem is that each of these choices seems wrong.
Against option 1, Dennett appeals to the fact that it seems like he would be able to survive a simple brain transplant operation, in which his brain is placed in a different body and his old body i

summary:
ACLU Complaint
Facts:
Plaintiff is an employee of Anchorage School District and is enrolled in the district's self funded PPO plan administered by AETNA ID: W1212-99544 GRP: 658742-31-013
Plaintiff suffers from Gender Dysphoria and requires gender affirming surgery as a medical necessity, this medical nesescity has been confirmed by 2 Psychologist and 1 Primary Care Physician.
The plaintiff has both gender affirming vaginoplasty and gender affirming breast augmentation surgery scheduled for 6/14/2023 and plaintiff's surgeon has requested preauthorization for the surgery.
AETNA as the administrator of the self-funded plan by the Anchorage School District has denied both required surgeries as having specific exclusions in the plan.
The Anchorage School District in Anchorage Alaska has a specific exclusion in place for "sexual enhancement" in their self funded health plan policy. This exclusion specifically excludes.  This is the exclusion used to deny me gender affirming surgery.
Any treatment, prescription drug, or supply to treat sexual dysfunction, enhance sexual performance or increase sexual desire, including: Surgery, prescription drugs, implants, devices or preparations to correct or enhance erectile function, enhance sensitivity or alter the shape of a sex organ Sex therapy, sex counseling, marriage counseling, or other counseling or advisory services..." 
The plaintiff has contacted the Benefits department of Anchorage School District with a request to remove or waive the blanket exclusion of gender affirming surgery.
The plaintiff has appealed with AETNA both denial
note: source-backed appeal example from live index

summary:
Instructions for using the Gender Affirming Surgery Appeal Template
Dear community member,
Thank you for using our online template to complete your health insurance appeal. To learn more about the health insurance appeal process, please visit our website at https://transhealthproject.org/
Please read these instructions before attempting to use the template:
Text highlighted in yellow should be replaced by information relevant to your own particular situation.
We have also included instructions & descriptions of the type of information you should be putting in each section, highlighted in green
Please check that all text highlighted in yellow & green has been replaced/deleted before you send the appeal.
You are free to add, edit, rearrange, or delete any portion of the appeal as you see fit.
For the insurance company information header, please look at either your denial letter or your plan policy’s section on appealing denials. This is usually at the end of the policy. It should include an address and the name of the administrator that the appeal should be addressed to.
If the denial letter and your plan policy give you different addresses, or you cannot find this information, please call your health plan or your HR department if you get your health insurance from your employer.
Information for the member section should be found in your denial letter. You can get this information from your medical provider if you cannot find it.
** Please note that the use of this template does not create an attorney – client relationship between TLDEF and you. **
Date
Sent via Fax/E-Mail/Ma

Downloading or saving claims

your search.

Appeals
Please send your written appeal along with a copy of this entire EOB to this address:
          Appeals Resolution Team

If you disagree with a claim decision, you can ask us to review it. The process is called an appeal. You or someone you name to act 
for you, your authorized representative, can ask for this review. Call our Member Services Department using the telephone number 
displayed on the member ID card or send your written request to the above address. 
Your request should include:
. Name, date of birth, and address
. Member ID number
. Group ID and name of your group, usually your employer 
. Any other claim documents or records or other facts you would like us to consider. This could be new details that you did not give 
us the first time.
You have the right to look at the relevant documents we used to make our decision on your claim. A copy of the specific rule,

Dear Kay Mayers, 
 
 This letter explains the terms of our relationship. Normally, we like to talk less formally. 
But a written agreement helps make sure we have gone over everything we need to. It also gives 
you something you can look back on. And TLDEF is required by law to put some of these things 
in writing.  
 
 Please read over everything below. If you are okay with all of it and want to agree,  please 
sign and return it to us.  If you have any questions, please ask. 
 
SCOPE OF REPRESENTATION 
 
1) I, Kay Mayers, (“Client”), retain the Transgender Legal Defense & Education Fund, Inc. 
(TLDEF) to represent me in this matter (the “matter”): 
 
To assist me with Aetna’s health insurance appeal process, 
including internal and external appeals, to contest the denial of 
coverage of my gender-affirming facial surgery 
 
2) I understand that this agreement describes our commitments and responsibilities.  
 
3) I authorize TLDEF to do whatever it deems necessary in good faith to represent me in this 
matter. 
 
4) I understand that TLDEF has not agreed to represent me in any other matter, or in anything 
beyond the internal and external health insurance appeals process of this matter. TLDEF and 

 
 
2 
I have discussed the possibility of TLDEF representing me in a lawsuit against Aetna about 
their policy excluding facial gender-affirming surgery from coverage. This agreement does 
not cover that lawsuit. If TLDEF and I decide to move forward on that lawsuit

summary:
Instructions for using the Gender Affirming Surgery Appeal Template
Dear community member,
Thank you for using our online template to complete your health insurance appeal.
To learn more about the health insurance appeal process, please visit our website at
https://transhealthproject.org/
Please read these instructions before attempting to use the template:
 Text highlighted in yellow should be replaced by information relevant to your own
particular situation.
 We have also included instructions & descriptions of the type of information you should
be putting in each section, highlighted in green
 Please check that all text highlighted in yellow & green has been replaced/deleted
before you send the appeal.
 You are free to add, edit, rearrange, or delete any portion of the appeal as you see fit.
 For the insurance company information header, please look at either your denial letter or
your plan policy’s section on appealing denials. This is usually at the end of the policy. It
should include an address and the name of the administrator that the appeal should be
addressed to.
 If the denial letter and your plan policy give you different addresses, or you cannot find
this information, please call your health plan or your HR department if you get your
health insurance from your employer.
 Information for the member section should be found in your denial letter. You can get
this information from your medical provider if you cannot find it.
** Please note that the use of this template does not create an
attorney – client relationship between TLDEF and you. **
Transgender L

To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing to appeal the denial of coverage for my medically necessary facial feminization surgery for the period of 04/11/2024 to 04/11/2025, as outlined in your letter dated 03/12/2024. The claim, associated with CPT codes 14040, 14301, 14302, 15773, 21121, 21137, 21138, 21139, and 21209, was unjustly denied under the pretext that the procedures are not medically necessary, an assertion starkly contradicted by a plethora of medical studies and legal precedents.
1. Medical Necessity and Personal Safety:
Your denial overlooks the vital role of facial feminization surgery in the lives of individuals diagnosed with gender dysphoria. This surgery is essential not only for my psychological well-being but also for my physical safety, given the prevalent discrimination and violence against transgender women.
Significant studies validate the necessity and efficacy of these surgeries:
Park RH et al. (2022) highlighted the long-term benefits of gender-affirming surgery, underscoring its life-saving potential (Ann Plast Surg. 89(4):431-436).
Dubov A, Fraenkel L (2018) discussed the ethical implications of gatekeeping in transgender health, emphasizing the medical necessity of facial feminization surgery (Am J Bioeth. 18(12):3-9).
Hauc SC et al. (2022) pointed out

summary:
To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing in response to your request for additional information regarding my billing dispute for the following charge:
Merchant: VIVID SEATS *NEW YORK
Amount: $781.26
Date of Charge: September 18, 2025 (approximate)
I am disputing this charge because I did not receive the merchandise or services associated with this transaction and did not knowingly participate in or authorize the purchase.
Description of the Dispute
I did not knowingly place an order with Vivid Seats and was not aware of this charge at the time it occurred. I did not receive any tickets, confirmations, QR codes, emails, or event details related to this transaction. As of today, no merchandise or services have been provided to me for the amount charged.
The charge appeared during a broader account compromise incident on September 18, 2025, during which my financial information was misused. I became aware of the Vivid Seats charge only after reviewing my account activity and immediately took steps to secure my accounts.
A police report documenting this incident and the related unauthorized charges was filed with the Anchorage Police Department.
Police Report Number: 250506465
Merchant Contact / Cancellation
Because I did not knowingly place this order and had no order confirmation, ticket details, or transaction reference information, I was unable to cancel the transaction or return merchandise under the merchant’s policy. I did not have sufficient information to contact the merchant regarding a specific order or cancellation.

To Whom It May Concern, 
I am writing in response to your request for additional information regarding my billing 
dispute for the following charge: 
Merchant: VIVID SEATS *CHRIS BRO 
Amount: $1,181.96 
Date of Charge: September 18, 2025 (approximate) 
I am disputing this charge because I did not receive the merchandise or services 
associated with this transaction and did not knowingly participate in or authorize the 
purchase. 
Description of the Dispute 
I did not knowingly place an order with Vivid Seats and was not aware of this charge at the 
time it occurred. I did not receive any tickets, conﬁrmations, QR codes, emails, or event 
details related to this transaction. As of today, no merchandise or services have been 
provided to me for the amount charged. 
The charge appeared during a broader account compromise incident on September 18, 
2025, during which my ﬁnancial information was misused. I became aware of the Vivid 
Seats charge only after reviewing my account activity and immediately took steps to secure 
my accounts. 
A police report documenting this incident and the related unauthorized charges was ﬁled 
with the Anchorage Police Department. 
Police Report Number: 250506465 
Merchant Contact / Cancellation 
Because I did not knowingly place this order and had no order conﬁrmation, ticket details, 
or transaction reference information, I was unable to cancel the transaction or return 
merchandise under the merchant’s policy. I did not have suƯicient information to contact 
the merchant regarding a speciﬁc order or cancellation. 
Merchandise / Service Details

The deductible and coinsurance amounts noted above are estimates based on the policy information given to us by your insurance carrier (i.e. whether or not your deductible has been met). These amounts do not include anesthesia fees or pathology fees. These are separate fees that you may receive a bill for after surgery.
The amount due to the Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center, or St. Francis Memorial Hospital following surgery may be significantly more or less than what was collected prior to surgery.
If we collected more than your portion, we will refund your credit card for the amount overpaid once we received payment from your insurance company.
If there is a balance due, you will receive a statement from the billing offices of either the Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center and/or St. Francis Memorial Hospital.
If you do receive a bill, please contact your insurance plan, as they will be best equipped to help you. If your insurance plan is unable to help you, please contact the billing office of the party that sent the statement (Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center and/or St. Francis Memorial Hospital).
Please note, once your claim has been processed by your insurance, you will receive documentation from your insurance company explaining your balance, which is called an Explanation of Benefits. You can use this document to verify either a statement or a refund.

summary:
The deductible and coinsurance amounts noted above are estimates based on the policy information given to us by your insurance carrier (i.e. whether or not your deductible has been met). These amounts do not include anesthesia fees or pathology fees. These are separate fees that you may receive a bill for after surgery.
The amount due to the Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center, or St. Francis Memorial Hospital following surgery may be significantly more or less than what was collected prior to surgery.
If we collected more than your portion, we will refund your credit card for the amount overpaid once we received payment from your insurance company.
If there is a balance due, you will receive a statement from the billing offices of either the Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center and/or St. Francis Memorial Hospital.
If you do receive a bill, please contact your insurance plan, as they will be best equipped to help you. If your insurance plan is unable to help you, please contact the billing office of the party that sent the statement (Gender Confirmation Center, Vista Surgery Center and/or St. Francis Memorial Hospital).
Please note, once your claim has been processed by your insurance, you will receive documentation from your insurance company explaining your balance, which is called an Explanation of Benefits. You can use this document to verify either a statement or a refund.
By signing below, you acknowledge that you have read and understood the above.

summary:
version: '3.8'

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    container_name: tichuml-db
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: tichuml
      POSTGRES_USER: tichuml
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: tichuml_dev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U tichuml"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  pgdata:

summary:
Aetna denied this claim based on that the policy does not cover transgender surgery, Our policy does in fact cover this. 

This claim was for pre-operative (donor site) hair removal before surgery. The surgery was approved pre-authorization #230516089159.

Since I was required to get pre-operative laser hair removal on the donor site, this procedure had to start before I went to surgery on June 14th, 2023.

Per the denial.
796 - Your plan does not cover services related to the treatment of gender identity disorder. [796]
 
Our plan BPD 

Employer: Anchorage School District
Contract number: MSA-0658742
Plan name: Open Choice Plan
Booklet: 1
Plan effective date: July 1, 2023
Plan issue date: June 30, 2023

Gender affirming treatment
Covered services include certain services and supplies for gender affirming (sometimes called sex change) 
treatment. 

Important note: 
Visit https://www.aetna.com/health-care-professionals/clinical-policy-bulletins.html for detailed information 
about this benefit, including eligibility and medical necessity requirements. You can also call the toll-free 
number on your ID card.

Very specifically the plan does cover gender identity disorder treatments and laser hair removal as a precondition of gender affirming surgery.
 
See CPB 0615 Gender Affirming Surgery - Medical Clinical Policy Bulletins | Aetna
 
Hair removal (e.g., electrolysis, laser hair removal) (Exception: A limited number of electrolysis or laser hair removal sessions are considered medically necessary for skin graft preparation for genital surgery)

1. To apply for a new U.S. passport book and/or card  please submit this form with a new DS-11, Application for a U.S. Passport , to any
designated acceptance facility, U.S. passport agency, U.S. embassy, or U.S. consulate abroad.
2. R
eport a lost or stolen U.S. passport online by visiting our website at travel.state.gov.
3. Report a lost or stolen U.S. passport by phone by calling 1-877-487-2778 (TDD: 1-888-874-7793) and following the representat ive's
instructions.
4. Submit this form by mail AND  a photocopy of a government-issued photo identification (such as a driver's license or state-issued
identification) to the following address* ONLY IF you are NOT applying for a new U.S. passport book and/or card:
AUTHORITIES: We are authorized to collect this information by 22 U.S.C. 211a et seq.; 8 U.S.C. 1104; 8 U.S.C. 1737; Executive Order 11295
(August 5, 1966); and 22 C.F.R. Parts 50 and 51.
PURPOSE: We are requesting the information to ensure that no one has more than one valid U.S. passport book and one valid U.S. passport card
at any one time, except as authorized by the U.S. Department of State, and to combat passport fraud and misuse. 
The collection of the Social Security number will be used to verify the identity of the person reporting the lost or stolen passport and to identify the
passport being reported lost or stolen. There is no other purpose unless authorized by law.
ROUTINE USES: This information may be disclosed to another domestic government agency, a private contractor, a foreign government agency, or
to a private person or private employer in accordance

Patient Financial Policy 
 
Please read this carefully and if you have any questions, please do not hesitate to ask a member of our staff. 
 
Thank you for choosing Alaska Women’s Health, PC. We are committed to providing you with quality health care. Your 
clear understanding of our Patient Financial Policy is important to our professional relationship. Please understand that 
payment for services is a part of that relationship. It is your responsibility to notify our office if any patient information 
changes (i.e., name, address, telephone, insurance information, etc.).  
 
Payment Policy & Insurance Filing: 
As a courtesy, AWH will file your primary and secondary insurance for you, if a copy of your insurance card(s) and a 
photo ID are provided at the time of service. If you are unable to provide this information, you will be required to pay for 
your visit at the time of service. We participate in most insurance plans, including Medicare. If you are not insured by a 
plan we participate with, payment in full is expected each visit. Knowing your insurance benefits is your responsibility. 
Please contact your insurance company with any questions you may have regarding your coverage. 
 
Co-payments and deductibles

and for the purpose of this evaluation is referred to as Kay and she/her.

Family of Origin. From infancy through age 8, Kay was raised in the back of an 
automotive parts store by her married parents, Pamela and Charles. When she was 8, 
her parents divorced and her mother began work as a cook in a county jail. Kay never 
again saw her father. Her mother remarried, to an inmate named Mark. Mark was 
released from jail and lived with Pamela and Kay for the remainder of Kay's childhood.

summary:
Weekly Study Plan Worksheet
Using the blank calendar on the next page and following the steps below, plan your weekly study schedule.
Block oﬀ existing obligations this week (i.e. work hours, childcare, family meal times, doctor’s appointment, etc)
Plan your fun ﬁrst! To help motivate your study time, schedule your distractions, exercise and relaxation time. This will give you something to look forward to.
Clearly mark any due dates or assignment deadlines, and use the GOALS list to remind yourself of the top tasks you need to complete this week or next.
Focus on tasks not time, and be as speciﬁc as possible. Review the activities you need to complete this week, estimate the time required for the task, and create a block for that task. For example, 25 minutes: watch video lecture, 25 minutes: create a practice test, 15 minutes: draft an outline for essay.
Use the pomodoro technique! When you begin a scheduled study block, work for 25-minute sessions followed by short breaks to keep your focus and energy going.
Pomodoro Technique
Pomodoro sets aside time for focusing on a speciﬁc task and rewards you with small chunks of time to be used for short breaks. There are six steps in the original technique:
Decide on the task to be done.
Set a timer for 25 minutes.
Work on the task.
End work when the timer rings and put a checkmark on a piece of paper.
If you have fewer than four checkmarks, take a short break (3–5 minutes), then go to step 2.
After four pomodoros, take a longer break (15–30 minutes), reset your checkmark count to zero, then go to step 1.

summary:
Weekly Study Plan Worksheet  
 
Using the blank calendar on the next page and following the steps below, plan your weekly study 
schedule. 
 
1. Block oﬀ existing obligations this week (i.e. work hours, childcare, family meal times, doctor’s 
appointment, etc) 
 
2. Plan your fun ﬁrst! To help motivate your study time, schedule your distractions, exercise 
and relaxation time. This will give you something to look forward to. 
 
3. Clearly mark any due dates or assignment deadlines, and use the GOALS list to remind 
yourself of the top tasks you need to complete this week or next. 
 
4. Focus on tasks not time, and be as speciﬁc as possible. Review the activities you need to 
complete this week, estimate the time required for the task, and create a block for that task. For 
example, 25 minutes: watch video lecture, 25 minutes: create a practice test, 15 minutes: draft 
an outline for essay. 
 
5. Use the pomodoro technique! When you begin a scheduled study block, work for 25-minute 
sessions followed by short breaks to keep your focus and energy going. 
 
 
Pomodoro Technique  
Pomodoro sets aside time for focusing on a speciﬁc task and rewards you with small chunks of time to 
be used for short breaks. There are six steps in the original technique: 
 
1. Decide on the task to be done. 
2. Set a timer for 25 minutes. 
3. Work on the task. 
4. End work when the timer rings and put a checkmark on a piece of paper. 
5. If you have fewer than four checkmarks, take a short break (3–5 minutes), then go to step 2. 
6. After four pomodoros, take a longer break (15–30 minutes), reset

This policy is designed to address these matters and is also designed to be used with a code ethics that further describes the policies and values of Identity.
Covered Persons

Affiliated Party

January 26, 2024 
Kay Mayers 
1941 Eastview Dr 
Anchorage, AK 99504 
Dear Kay: 
I am writing in response to your request for assistance from the American Civil Liberties 
Union of Alaska. We want to thank you for taking the time to contact us. 
Unfortunately, the ACLU of Alaska is unable to assist you in this matter. As a private, non-
profit organization with limited resources, we must be very selective in choosing our cases, 
and can afford to take only a small percentage of the many meritorious complaints that we 
receive. Our inability to represent you in this matter is not a judgment on the merits of 
your case; we simply do not have the resources to assist you at this time. 
You may lose your right to pursue a claim if you do not file a lawsuit or administrative 
complaint before certain deadlines. If you wish to pursue this matter, we recommend that 
you contact a private attorney or another legal services organization. 
Enclosed please find information on other public interest legal organizations and on the 
Alaska Bar Association's Lawyer Referral Service. Unfortunately, we cannot provide 
individual referrals to specific attorneys or organizations. 
We wish you the best in resolving your situation. 
Sincerely, 
ACLU of Alaska team 
End.

Name Change Process
• A copy of your social security card showing your new legal name is required. After completing this form, 
please email a copy of the card to benefitsdept@asdk12.org.
• If your name change is due to marriage or divorce, please complete this Marital Status Change form 
and complete the other related benefits and retirement action items listed here. 
Human Resources will process your name change within two weeks of receipt of both this form and new social 
security card. We will then forward the request to the IT Service Desk to update your email and logins. 
Please answer the following questions for the IT Service Desk:
1. Do you want your email address and system logins updated?
2. Have you created an ASD web page?
3. Do you use Business Plus (IFAS)?
4. What kind of computer do you use?
IT will email you just before they start the change process. When you log in with your new name, if the computer’s 
desktop or files do not appear the same as before, please call the IT Service Desk at 742-4615.

SURGEON: Dr. Sven Gunther
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal.
INDICATIONS: The patient is a 51 year old female who has followed
the WPATH standards of care. She has a good understanding of the surgical
procedure as well as expectations and potential complications and wishes to
proceed. Consent obtained.
OPERATIVE FINDINGS: See admission history and physical.
OPERATIVE PROCEDURE: After informed consent was obtained, the patient was
taken to the operating room where a general endotracheal anesthetic was
administered. She was placed in the dorsal lithotomy position and prepped and
draped in the usual sterile fashion. A butterfly-shaped incision was marked
from the midline perineum in a cephalad direction to 90 degrees from the penis
along the scrotal skin on each side. The midline was also marked. The skin
was excised and then prepared separately as a full-thickness skin graft by
removing the underlying fat and connective tissue and depilating the
individual hair follicles later in the procedure with electrocautery. The full thickness scrotal graft 
was then sewn to the inverted penile skin with 2-0 Vicryl later in the procedure.
 
Each testicle was grasped with a towel clamp, then tracted and dissection used to dissect down to
the inguinal ring bilaterally. Each spermatic cord was ligated with 0-Vicryl suture, then divided.

About this trip
Anchorage Yoga is excited to present an exclusive retreat—an offering like never before. This unique
experience, with its profound transformative potential, focuses on healing through music and earth
medicines. It's designed to guide you into the depths of your heart, where you can repair and
reconnect with your most authentic self: love. This journey promises to inspire hope and ignite
transformation.
Anchorage Yoga's Medicine & Music Retreat will occur from March 29th to April 4th at Finca El Pendulo
in Jalisco, México. A unique feature of this retreat is an optional music festival on the night of the 4th.
This festival, showcasing incredible Bhakti musicians, provides an enchanting conclusion to our
transformational retreat. It offers a unique opportunity to further immerse yourself in the healing power
Trip Overview

of music, a key element of our retreat's transformative potential.

To be submitted by the referring physician
Patient information
Date: _____________
Patient name: _______________________________________
Patient phone: (____)_______-____________
Patient date of birth: _____/_______/_______
Most Blue Cross and Aetna plans will cover hyperbaric oxygen therapy
(HBOT) to treat the following on label conditions:
DIAGNOSIS(ES) and ICD-10 CODES
☐ Diabetic Non-Healing Wound ICD-10 code: E11.621
☐ Soft Tissue Radionecrosis ICD-10 code: L59.8
☐ Osteoradionecrosis ICD-10 code: M27.2

T86.821
☐ Thermal Burns
☐ Crush Injuries ICD-10 code:
☐ Acute Traumatic Peripheral Ischemia
☐ Acute Peripheral Arterial Insufficiency
☐ Acute Carbon Monoxide Poisoning ICD-10 code: T58.91XA
☐ Cyanide Poisoning
A number of commercial insurance companies will approve HBOT for off
label conditions if they deem treatment as medically necessary:
☐ Addiction Recovery
☐ Alzheimer's
☐ Arthritis
☐ Autism
☐ Burn Treatment
☐ Cerebral Palsy
☐ Chronic Fatigue Syndrome

☐ Migraine
☐ Mold Toxicity

  "private": true,
  "workspaces": [
    "packages/*"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "concurrently \"npm run dev:server\" \"npm run dev:client\"",
    "dev:server": "npm run dev --workspace=packages/server",
    "dev:client": "npm run dev --workspace=packages/client",
    "build": "npm run build --workspaces",
    "test": "npm run test --workspace=packages/engine",
    "test:all": "npm run test --workspaces --if-present",
    "migrate": "npm run migrate --workspace=packages/server",
    "seed": "npm run seed --workspace=packages/server",
    "sim": "npm run sim --workspace=packages/sim",
    "replay": "npm run replay --workspace=packages/sim"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "concurrently": "^8.2.2",
    "typescript": "^5.4.0"
  }
}

summary:
# TichuML

A production-minded full-stack Tichu game platform for play, simulation, telemetry, and future ML training.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│   Client     │◄──►│   Server     │◄──►│  PostgreSQL  │
│  React+Pixi  │    │  Express API │    │  Telemetry   │
└──────┬───────┘    └──────┬───────┘    └──────────────┘
       │                   │
       ▼                   ▼
┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│   Engine     │    │   Sim/CLI    │
│  Pure Logic  │    │  AI Policies │
└─────────────┘    └──────────────┘
```

### Packages

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `@tichuml/shared` | Shared types, action definitions, telemetry schemas |
| `@tichuml/engine` | Pure deterministic game engine — no DB, no React |
| `@tichuml/server` | Express API, DB persistence, simulation orchestration |
| `@tichuml/client` | React + PixiJS frontend — play client & debug viewer |
| `@tichuml/sim` | AI policies, batch simulation runner, CLI tools |

## Quick Start

```bash
# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start Postgres (optional — app works in-memory without it)
docker compose up -d

# Run migrations (requires Postgres)
npm run migrate

# Start dev server + client
npm run dev

# Or start individually:
npm run dev:server  # API on :3001
npm run dev:client  # Client on :5173
```

## Simulation

```bash
# Run AI vs AI simulation
npm run sim -- --games 20 --seed 42 --target 500

# Specify policies per seat
npm run sim -- --games 100 --p0 heuristic --p1 random --p2 heuristic --p3 random

# Available policies: random, heuristic
`

summary:
During the agreed upon installation of a new heating system started on December 3rd at 1941 East View Dr, Anchorage AK 99504, highland mechanical was unable to complete the job in the 3 days that they were onsite and now are unable to continue the job until the 14th of December due to the owner’s personal obligations.
Due to these circumstances, Highland Mechanical agrees to the following
Providing 8 Electric Heaters and placing appropriately and configuring in the house for maximum safety.
A $500 Discount on the remaining balance of $4200.
Will accept responsibility for any costs incurred during the time that heating remains off in the house and the job remains incomplete, including services from 3rd parties to repair any damages caused due to lack of heat to the property if Highland Mechanical is not available.
Will pay the difference in electrical bill due to having to run 8 standalone electric heaters 24/7 for the duration or an additional year of labor warranty on top of the original year of labor warranty for a total of 2 years.
Will run new thermostat wire to make the new system compatible with our existing thermostat.
Will complete the job on December 14th-16th, 2020 or will pay for a 3rd party to do an emergency completion of the job.
This was agreed to by Donavon Colbry on December 5th, 2020.
_____________________________________         ________________
Donovan Colbry                                                             December 5th, 2020.
Owner Operator of Highland Mechanical

Re:  Aetna and Anchorage School District 
  AKDOI File #23-124-87200-DB (Please refer to on all correspondence) 
 
Dear Kay Mayers, 
 
The Division of Insurance received your letter of complaint against AETNA. The Anchorage School 
District (ASD) health plan is a self-funded employee welfare benefit plan established and sponsored 
by the ASD with AETNA acting as the third-party plan administrator.  The division has no 
regulatory authority over self-funded (also called self-insured) employee welfare benefit plans.  This 
benefit plan is not subject to state insurance regulation nor is this benefit plan subject to regulation 
under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 (ERISA).    
 
The employer’s plan booklet should provide information on how to file complaints regarding the 
group health benefits.  We suggest that you contact the employer or your Union (as applicable) with 
any other questions.  At your discretion, you may inquire with CMS (Centers for Medicare and 
Medicaid Services) as to whether the encountered issue is subject to the Public Health Service (PHS) 
Act or the The Affordable Care Act.  Their e-mail address is NonFed@cms.hhs.gov.  
    
Feel free to contact us with future concerns that may fall within our regulatory authority. 
 
 
S

Create a 60-second cinematic video titled “Public Health Nurse Recruitment Pitch.”
Use warm, golden morning lighting, soft cinematic visuals, and an inspirational female narrator. 
Add smooth transitions, motivational background music, and clear on-screen captions for each scene. 
Keep the tone professional, compassionate, and hopeful.
Include the following scenes:
Scene 1 (0:00–0:10) — The Calling
Visual: A nurse walking toward a small community clinic in the morning sunlight.
On-screen text: “Some nurses heal in hospitals; public health nurses heal entire communities.”
Voiceover: “Some nurses heal in hospitals, but public health nurses heal entire communities — meeting people where they live, work, and grow.”
Scene 2 (0:11–0:20) — The Role
Visual: A nurse talking with families and patients in a welcoming clinic.
On-screen text: “Promoting wellness. Preventing disease. Protecting populations.”
Voiceover: “Public Health Nurses promote wellness, prevent disease, and protect entire populations before illness even begins.”
Scene 3 (0:21–0:35) — Education and Growth
Visual: Nursing students studying together or practicing skills in training.
On-screen text: “From nursing foundations to public health leadership.”
Voiceover: “Start with nursing foundations, then grow into public health leadership — combining clinical skills with community care.”


Scene 4 (0:36–0:50) — Impact and Demand
Visual: Nurses helping at health fairs, community events, and home visits.
On-screen text: “High demand. Real impact. Beyond hospitals.”
Voiceover: “Public Health Nurses are in high demand — makin

First off, I suspect I am familiar with your sense of humor. Everyone on 
my mom's side is British. And so I sort of was weaned on dry humor and 
silly television like Are You Being Served, Faulty Towers, and Red 
Dwarf. And since it is the season I've got to tell you right off I DO NOT 
LIKE MINCE PIE AT ALL - not in the slightest. Not even a little bit. I’d 
rather eat my running shoes, and I’ll just leave it up to your imagination 
as to how absolutely horrid that would be – though it would not even hold 
a candle to the thought of eating a mince pie. 
 
Moving on, I do have tattoos, ones my namesake and the other is my 
star sign. Both reflect my personality and general approach to life. I’ll let 
you guess what they are. 
 
As far as favorite movies, hmmm... that’s a tough one - we might have to 
exchange notes on that topic. My taste run from brutal stuff involving 
destruction on a massive scale to twisted artsy flicks like Clockwork 
Orange and that genre. Really whatever mood I'm in at the time. I also 
like Shakespeare, Broadway, and music performances; I am not claiming 
to be a "Shakespear

summary:
GCC Facial Surgery Consultation – Returning Patient (1-Page Guide)
Opening
“It’s good to see you again. I really appreciated the work you did previously, and I wanted to come back to you specifically because you already know my anatomy and surgical history.”
Purpose
“I’m here mainly to get your perspective. I don’t have a fixed procedure list — I want your honest assessment of what would make sense and what wouldn’t.”
Current Areas of Concern
“There are a few areas I’ve been thinking about since my last surgery.”
Philtrum — feels long relative to the lower face
Cheeks — wondering about midface support/softness rather than volume
Eyebrows — peak sits a bit low
Left eye — persistent fat/fullness that hasn’t resolved
“I’m especially interested in what you see as structural versus soft tissue.”
Boundaries / Priorities
“I’m not looking to overcorrect or chase perfection. Balance, function, and still looking like myself are important to me.”
Invite Surgical Judgment
“Based on knowing my face and the work you’ve already done, what do you think would have the most meaningful impact?”
Optional follow-ups (use only if needed):
“Are any of these things you’d recommend not addressing?”
“Would you think in terms of staging or a single revision?”
“Are there tradeoffs here I should understand?”

summary:
Identity Integration & Embodiment — One■Page Summary
Core Experience
You are a trans woman whose external reality is female, while your nervous system still carries old identity memory.
This creates checking, self■monitoring, and doubt — not because you are unsure, but because survival habits persist
after safety is achieved.
Identity Memory vs Reality
Reality: You are read as a woman — with makeup, without makeup, at rest, and in daily life.
Identity memory: An old alarm that asks you to check again.
This alarm is memory, not masculinity. Thoughts, habits, movement, and cognition are not gendered — people are.
Movement & Thinking
There is no single way women move, dance, or think. Stiffness, analytical thinking, reserve, or directness do not signal
masculinity. What limits fluidity is vigilance, not identity. Ease returns through safety, not performance.
Ketamine Work
Ketamine helps quiet self■monitoring and loosen rigid narratives. Its value lies in allowing moments of simply “being”
without checking. Integration focuses on remembering what safety felt like, not forcing insight or change.
Sexual Desire & Timing
Wanting sexual experience as a woman is about embodiment and being seen, not betrayal or replacement. You are not
late; you are claiming a chapter that could not happen earlier.
Orgasm & Pleasure Retraining
Post■op orgasm difficulty is usually nervous■system based, not mechanical. The capacity exists. Pressure and
monitoring block it. Pleasure retraining emphasizes sensation, curiosity, safety, and removing performance goals.
Orgasm returns as a byproduct, not a ta

Anchorage Yoga is excited to present an exclusive retreat—an offering like never before. This unique
experience, with its profound transformative potential, focuses on healing through music and earth
medicines. It's designed to guide you into the depths of your heart, where you can repair and
reconnect with your most authentic self: love. This journey promises to inspire hope and ignite
transformation.
Anchorage Yoga's Medicine & Music Retreat will occur from March 29th to April 4th at Finca El Pendulo

Kay Mayers
Professor Johnson
PHIL A201

The Problem of Evil: A Comparison of Stump and Rowe
During the first few weeks of this semester we have looked at several philosophical topics including logic, deductive reasoning, philosophy of religion, the problem of evil, and epistemology. Out of all of these, the problem of evil feels like one of the most difficult and uncomfortable. The basic issue is simple to state: how can an all-powerful and perfectly good God exist if there is so much suffering in the world? At first glance, those ideas do not seem to fit together easily. If God has both the power to prevent suffering and the desire to do so, then the continued presence of suffering seems like something that needs explaining.
Eleonore Stump argues that suffering does not automatically create a contradiction between God’s goodness and the existence of evil. In her view, we should not assume that goodness always means eliminating suffering right away. She suggests that suffering may serve a purpose, even if we cannot see that purpose clearly. Some forms of suffering might contribute to moral or spiritual growth. From this perspective, a perfectly good being might allow suffering if it leads to a greater good in the long run. Just because we do not see that good does not mean it is not there.
William Rowe approaches the issue differently. He does not claim that God and evil logically contradict each other. Instead, he argues that certain cases of suffering count as evidence against the existence of a perfectly good and all-powerful God. His well-known example o

summary:
Kay Mayers
Professor Johnson
PHIL A201
27 February 2026
The Problem of Evil: A Comparison of Stump and Rowe
During the first few weeks of this semester we have looked at several philosophical topics including logic, deductive reasoning, philosophy of religion, the problem of evil, and epistemology. One of the most persistent questions in philosophy of religion is whether the existence of evil can fit with the existence of an all-powerful and perfectly good God. At first glance, these ideas seem difficult to hold together. If God has both the power and the desire to prevent suffering, then the continued presence of suffering still demands some kind of explanation.
Eleonore Stump argues that suffering does not create a logical contradiction between God’s goodness and the reality of evil because suffering may serve purposes beyond our limited understanding. William Rowe, in “The Problem of Evil and Some Varieties of Atheism,” does not claim that God and evil cannot both be true. Instead, he argues that certain cases of suffering provide evidence against God’s existence. I will argue that while Stump blocks the claim that God and evil are logically incompatible, Rowe’s argument based on probability remains a serious challenge because the repeated appearance of apparently pointless suffering cannot simply be ignored.
Stump argues that suffering may serve a purpose even when that purpose is not immediately obvious to us. Goodness is not the same thing as eliminating all suffering. Some forms of suffering may contribute to moral or spiritual growth in ways that are hard to see fro

summary:
Kay Mayers
Professor Johnson
PHIL A201
1 March 2026
The Problem of Evil: A Logical and Evidential Comparison
During the first few weeks of this semester we have examined several philosophical topics including logic, deductive reasoning, philosophy of religion, the problem of evil, and epistemology. One of the most persistent questions that arises in philosophy of religion is whether the existence of evil is compatible with the existence of an all-powerful and perfectly good God. At first glance, these ideas seem difficult to hold together. If God has the power to prevent suffering and also has the desire to do so, then it becomes unclear why suffering continues to exist in the world at all.
In J. L. Mackie’s “Evil and Omnipotence,” he argues that the traditional conception of God is logically inconsistent when combined with the existence of evil. William Rowe, in his article “The Problem of Evil and Some Varieties of Atheism,” approaches the issue somewhat differently. Rowe does not argue that God and evil are logically incompatible, but instead that the existence of certain kinds of suffering makes God’s existence unlikely. In the first part of this essay I will compare Mackie’s logical problem of evil with Rowe’s evidential problem of evil. In the second part I will explain my own view.
Part I: Comparing Mackie and Rowe
Mackie’s argument can be expressed in deductive form. First, if God exists then God is omnipotent and perfectly good. Second, a perfectly good being would eliminate evil so far as it could. Third, an omnipotent being could eliminate any evil that is logica
note: source-backed form example from live index

Document info
Result type: Nursing Progress Note
Result date: Apr 09, 2025, 03:35 a.m.
Result status: authenticated
Performed by: Joy Anne Santiago
Verified by: Joy Anne Santiago
Modified by: Joy Anne Santiago
Patient: MAYERS, KAY DOB: May 19, 1972
52 y/o pt, S/P Facial Feminization. ( see Op Report ) by Dr. Facque, 4/8/25
   
Received A/Ox4, calm and pleasant. VSS, on room air. ATC pain management overnight with Dilaudid
and Percocet, pain varies 5-8/10.
Head drsg intact along forehead and neck, nasal drip pad intact. JP on L side head, drained
10cc..Periorbital hematoma and swelling. Cold saline patch applied. Abdomen with puncture site, skin
glued, OTA.
Foley cath drained c/a/uo. Discontinued at 0530. Awaiting to void at this time.
+ BS, + flatulence. No N/V.
PIV on LAC, IVF infusing well, Unasyn administered.
On Soft bite size texture.
Up to chair at 0600.
   
POC: Observation pt. Discharge today.
Pt was looking for her Nurtec medication. Can't be found on the floor. Please call PACU/CGU to confirm.

I received a call saying he was uber support saying he knew I was driving to my next pick up but he had received a customer
complaint that I didn’t match my profile in the app so they needed to verify.He then got me to send pictures through text and
then told me to get online and he would stay on call to get the passengers to verify I was the person in their app that was
supposed to pick them up. I also stupidly clicked the share screen on my phone.During the ride he would have codes sent
and I was stupid and answered, it was distracting so I didn’t pay enough attention and they were a couple of credit card verify
codes. After my ride ended I questioned one of the codes and he said it was to send me the uber sign for the car.I started
feeling like it was a scam and he threatened to deactivate

REASON FOR REFERRAL 
Kay Mayers of Anchorage, Alaska, requested a psychological evaluation to determine 
whether she experiences Autism Spectrum Disorder and to formulate an actionable 
treatment plan. She has previously been diagnosed with ADHD, Anxiety, and 
Depression. Kay transitioned from male to female at age 50. Her dead name is "Keif' 
and for the purpose of this evaluation is referred to as Kay and she/her. 
BACKGROUND 
Family of Origin. From infancy through age 8, Kay was raised in the back of an 
automotive parts store by her married parents, Pamela and Charles. When she was 8, 
her parents divorced and her mother began work as a cook in a county jail. Kay never 
again saw her father. Her mother remarried, to an inmate named Mark. Mark was 
released from jail and lived with Pamela and Kay for the remainder of Kay's childhood. 
Childhood Experiences. "My school experience can be summarized as awful! No 
social skills. Fights." Kay reports that she "threw tantrums." She had no dependable 
friends. "I never fit in with the other boys. I got along better with the girls in the 
neighborhood." Kay earned generally poor grades. She excelled in academics only 
when interested in th

the claim of a universal midlife crisis is false. (Correct)
the majority of women experience this as a result of the empty nest, but it is much less likely to be experienced by men because they are more likely to work outside the home.
the majority of the American population experiences it either in their 40s or 50s.
only those from ethnic minority backgrounds experience it because of more stress and discrimination than their counterparts from majority backgrounds.

I respectfully ask that this exclusion of coverage for gender dysphoria treatment be removed from the health plan. Over the past few years, similar exclusions have been largely eliminated in public programs and private insurance, as medical experts, state and federal agencies, and the business community developed a greater understanding of the medical benefit and cost effectiveness of providing this treatment. Courts have also found that these types of exclusions are discriminatory and violate federal law.
It is the overwhelming consensus among major medical organizations that transition-related treatments, including counseling, hormone therapy and surgical treatments, are safe, effective, and medically necessary when clinically indicated to treat gender dysphoria. Major medical organizations such as the American Medical Association, the American College of Physicians, the American Psychological Association, the American Psychiatric Association, the American Academy of Family Physicians, the Endocrine Society, the American Academy of Pediatrics, and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists, have issued policy statements to this effect, and numerous studies have demonstrated the significant benefits of medical treatments for gender dysphoria. Template Letter from You to Your Employer or School Asking Them to Remove an Exclusion
[FOR SELF-FUNDED PLANS WITH EXCLUSIONS ONLY]
INSTRUCTIONS: 
Only use this template if you have a self-funded plan that has an exclusion. To understand whether you have a self-funded plan, and for resources on approaching your employer, please refer to this page.
Replace all the bold text with your information.
The section that says “WHY REMOVING THE EXCLUSION IS IMPORTANT FOR YOU AND OTHER EMPLOYERS OR STUDENTS” is optional. You can use the space to include additional information about why it would be important for you or other employers or students that employers/students have access to transition-related care, or to a specific procedure that is currently excluded. If discussing a specific procedure, it would be helpful to include statements from medical organizations or studies. You can refer to the health care provider’s letter page for more resources.
Keep in mind that you can consider reaching out to other colleagues who may want to co-sign the letter and support you in this process, including non-transgender colleagues.
If your employer or school continues to refuse to remove the exclusion after you send this letter, we strongly recommend that you reach out to a lawyer to discuss other options (you can refer to this list of legal resources).
